title: Why the return of desire and wanting feels terrifying
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
You finally feel safe enough to want something. And it's terrifying. 

 I remember the exact moment it happened. I was sitting on my couch, scrolling thro...
htmlContent:
You finally feel safe enough to want something. And it's terrifying.

I remember the exact moment it happened. I was sitting on my couch, scrolling through my phone, and a thought drifted in. Quiet. Almost polite. I want to paint again.

My body reacted before my mind could catch up. My chest tightened. My throat closed. My hands went cold. I felt a wave of nausea that came from nowhere.

And I thought: what the hell is wrong with me?

Here's the thing. I had spent years in survival mode. Decades, really. My nervous system had learned one thing, and it had learned it well: wanting things is dangerous. Wanting leads to disappointment. Wanting leads to loss. Wanting leads to the kind of pain that breaks you open and leaves you hollow.

So my system built a fortress. It shut down desire before desire could hurt me. It killed wanting before wanting could kill me. It was a survival strategy. And it worked.

Until it didn't.

Because here I was, finally safe enough that my nervous system was starting to unclench. And with that unclenching came something I hadn't felt in years. Wanting. Desire. The quiet whisper of I want something for myself.

And I was terrified.

I'm not alone in this. If you're reading this, you might know exactly what I'm talking about. You might feel it in your body right now. That strange mix of hope and dread that comes when something inside you starts to wake up.

Stay with me here. We're going to talk about why this happens. What it means. And what you can do when the return of desire feels less like liberation and more like a threat.

The nervous system doesn't trust hope

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, would tell you that your nervous system has a job. One job. Keep you alive. That's it. Your nervous system doesn't care if you're happy. It doesn't care if you're fulfilled. It cares if you're safe.

And here's the problem. Your nervous system learned, through years of experience, that desire is not safe. Wanting something means opening yourself up to the possibility of not getting it. Wanting something means vulnerability. Wanting something means risk.

Think about it. When you were a kid, did you want something and get punished for it? Did you want attention and get ignored? Did you want love and get hurt? Did you want safety and get chaos?

Your nervous system remembers. It remembers every single time wanting led to pain. And it built a firewall. A protective shutdown that kept you from feeling desire at all.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body. It's not just a memory in your head. It's a pattern in your nervous system. A template. And that template says: desire equals danger.

So when you start to feel safe enough to want something, your nervous system doesn't celebrate. It sounds the alarm. It says: Oh no. We've been here before. This is how we get hurt. Shut it down. Shut it down now.

That panic you feel? That nausea? That sudden urge to retreat? That's not weakness. That's your nervous system doing its job. It's trying to protect you from a threat it learned about a long time ago.

The problem is that the threat isn't there anymore. The danger is past. But your nervous system doesn't know that. It's still running the old program.

And that's why the return of desire feels terrifying. Because your body is reacting to a ghost.

Desire is a door you've been trained not to open

Here's something that doesn't get talked about enough. In many trauma survivors, desire doesn't just disappear. It gets locked away. It gets buried. It gets hidden behind walls so thick that you forget it was ever there.

And those walls? They were built for a reason. They were built because wanting something meant you had something to lose. And when you've lost everything, when you've been hurt so deeply that you thought you wouldn't survive, you learn to stop wanting. You learn to stop reaching. You learn to stop hoping.

It's a survival strategy. And it's a smart one. But here's the thing about survival strategies. They don't know when to stop. They keep running long after the danger is gone.

I worked with a client once who told me she couldn't even let herself want a cup of coffee. She'd stand in front of the coffee shop, feeling her body tense up, and then she'd walk away. She couldn't name what she wanted. She couldn't even allow herself to want something as simple as a drink.

That's what complex trauma does. It doesn't just take away big desires. It takes away small ones too. It takes away the everyday wanting that makes life feel alive.

And when those desires start to come back, it feels like a threat. Because your system doesn't know how to handle them. It only knows how to handle the absence of desire. The numbness. The safety of not wanting.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's work on emotional flashbacks and the freeze response helps explain why your system might be reacting so strongly to the return of desire.

Because here's the truth. That terror you feel? It's not about what you want. It's about what your system learned about wanting in the first place.

The shame spiral that follows desire

Let's talk about what happens after the panic. Because for many of us, the return of desire doesn't just trigger fear. It triggers shame.

You feel that flicker of wanting. Maybe it's wanting to create something. Maybe it's wanting to connect with someone. Maybe it's wanting to take up space in the world. And then, almost immediately, the shame arrives.

Who do you think you are?

You don't deserve that.

You'll just mess it up anyway.

What makes you think you can have that?

That voice. You know that voice. It's loud. It's cruel. And it sounds like it's telling the truth.

But here's what I've learned. That voice isn't truth. It's protection. It's a part of you that learned, a long time ago, that the safest way to avoid disappointment is to never let yourself want in the first place.

Richard Schwartz, the founder of Internal Family Systems, would call that voice a protector part. It's not your enemy. It's a part of you that's trying to keep you safe. It just doesn't know that the danger is over.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link). Schwartz's work helps you understand that even the parts of you that seem cruel or judgmental are actually trying to protect you.

And that shame spiral? It's not a sign that something is wrong with you. It's a sign that something happened to you. Your system learned to attack desire before desire could attack you.

But here's the hard part. That shame spiral can keep you stuck. It can keep you in the numbness. It can keep you from ever reaching for what you want.

And that's a kind of prison. A safe prison, maybe. But a prison nonetheless.

How to be with the terror without running

So what do you do when desire shows up and your body screams no?

First, you stop trying to make it go away. That's the instinct, right? To push the desire down. To distract yourself. To go back to the safety of numbness.

But that just reinforces the old pattern. Your system learns that desire is dangerous because every time it shows up, you run from it.

Instead, try something different. Try staying. Try being with the terror without trying to fix it.

Here's what that looks like in practice. You notice the desire. Maybe it's a tiny flicker. Maybe it's a full-blown want. And you notice the fear that comes with it. The tightness in your chest. The knot in your stomach. The voice that tells you to stop.

And instead of listening to that voice, you just breathe. You put a hand on your chest. You say, I see you. I know you're scared. I'm not going to act on this desire right now. I'm just going to let it be here.

That's it. You don't have to do anything with the desire. You don't have to pursue it. You don't have to act on it. You just have to let it exist.

This is where the body work comes in. Because the terror isn't in your head. It's in your body. And if you can learn to be with the physical sensations of fear without running, your nervous system starts to learn something new. It starts to learn that desire can be present without danger.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link) by Oprah Winfrey and Dr. Bruce Perry. It's a reminder that your reactions aren't signs of brokenness. They're signs of adaptation.

And that's the key. Your terror is adaptation. Your shame is adaptation. Your numbness is adaptation. All of it was brilliant. All of it kept you alive.

But you don't need to be in survival mode anymore. And the return of desire, as terrifying as it is, is actually a sign that your system is starting to trust again. It's starting to believe that maybe, just maybe, it's safe enough to want something.

That's huge. That's terrifying. And that's also beautiful.

I'm not going to tell you that the fear goes away quickly. It doesn't. For me, it's been years, and I still feel that catch in my throat when I let myself want something real. But I've learned to be with it. I've learned to say, Yes, I'm scared. And I'm still going to let this desire exist.

Sit with that. Let it land.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link) by Tara Brach. Her work on turning toward our experience with compassion, rather than judgment, can be a lifeline when the shame spiral hits.

You don't have to be ready to pursue your desires. You just have to be willing to let them exist. To let them breathe. To let them be part of your inner without immediately shutting them down.

That's enough. That's more than enough. That's the beginning.

And the beginning, as terrifying as it is, is also the place where healing lives.

Healing Journey Resources

  • What Happened to You? (paid link) - A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) - A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate
  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) - A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection
